title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
concept: RAG connects LLMs to external databases, letting them retrieve and read updated private documents before answering, eliminating hallucination.

exercises.prompt: Why do standard AI models sometimes make up facts when asked about recent events?

options.text: They do not have access to real-time or private information.
They forget how to speak English over time.
They are not allowed to read user questions.

exercises.explain: Static training data means standard LLMs cannot know about events or private files created after they were trained.

cards.title: AI's Memory Limit
cards.body: Even the smartest AI models can't know everything, especially private data or facts that changed yesterday. When they don't know, they often confidently make things up! How do we give them access to the real-time facts they need?

cards.title: What is RAG?
cards.body: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that connects an LLM to an external knowledge source. Before answering, the system retrieves relevant documents from a database and feeds them to the LLM as context, ensuring accurate answers.

cards.title: The Open-Book Exam
cards.body: Imagine an open-book exam. Instead of relying purely on what they memorized last semester (the LLM's static training), the student gets to search a library shelf (the database), pull out the exact book containing the facts, and write a perfect, up-to-date answer.

cards.title: RAG in Action
cards.body: A company plugs its private HR handbook database into a RAG system. When an employee asks, 'What is our parental leave policy?', the system searches the handbook, finds the exact 2024 policy clause, and feeds it to the LLM to generate a clear, custom reply.

cards.title: No Retraining Needed
cards.body: Many think LLMs must be completely retrained or 'fine-tuned' to learn new private documents. In fact, RAG lets you update the AI's answers instantly just by updating the database it reads from—no expensive model retraining required!

cards.title: Comparing Approaches
cards.body: See how RAG changes how an AI finds the information it needs to answer your question.

left.text: Relies only on past memory. Might guess or hallucinate if it doesn't know.

right.text: Looks up the latest database files first, then summarizes the facts.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary benefit of using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)?

options.text: To make the AI run much faster on older computers.
To provide up-to-date facts and stop the AI from making up false information.
To teach the AI how to speak different human languages.
To completely replace the LLM with a simple search engine.

exercises.explain: Hallucination happens when AI makes up false facts because it lacks access to the correct data. RAG solves this by feeding the AI the real documents to read.

exercises.prompt: True or False: RAG can be used to let an AI securely answer questions about private company files.
exercises.explain: RAG allows you to connect the AI to private databases, internal documents, or live web feeds without sharing them publicly.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step of the RAG workflow.
exercises.sentence: When a user asks a question, the RAG system first [blank] matching document snippets and then sends them to the LLM.
exercises.explain: RAG first searches (retrieves) documents from an external source, then uses that text to generate the final response.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI assistants is using RAG to answer a user's question about live train delays?

options.text: Assistant 1: 'I think the 5:00 PM train is running, but I do not have access to live schedules to verify.'
Assistant 2: 'Based on the live rail database I just searched, the 5:00 PM train is delayed by 15 minutes.'
Assistant 3: 'Train schedules are complex. Would you like me to write a poem about railways instead?'

exercises.explain: The second assistant is using RAG: it pulled the actual, real-time schedule from an external source (the current train schedule database) to answer accurately.

exercises.prompt: How does Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) combine databases with an LLM to answer a prompt?

options.text: It automatically retrains the entire core model from scratch every time a user asks a question.
It translates the user's prompt into programming code and executes it on a server.
It retrieves matching document facts first, then hands them to the LLM to generate an answer.
It blocks the LLM from writing anything and instead only displays raw database tables.

exercises.explain: RAG works by first searching external documents for relevant facts, and then feeding those facts to the generator (the LLM) so it can write an accurate, grounded response.
